name: Kong Gateway Admin API
what_it_does: Exposes a REST admin API to configure Kong API Gateway — managing routes, services, consumers, plugins (rate limiting, auth, logging), and upstreams programmatically.
use_cases: Dynamically register new agent service endpoints as Kong routes during deployment without manual gateway config changes
Apply rate limiting plugins to specific routes to protect backend agent APIs from traffic spikes
Create per-consumer API keys and attach usage quotas so different agents or tenants have isolated rate limits
Configure JWT or key-auth plugins on routes to enforce authentication before requests reach agent backends
Query Kong's built-in metrics and node status to monitor gateway health as part of an agent observability pipeline
not_for: Managed API gateway with no operational overhead (use Zuplo, AWS API Gateway, or Azure API Management)
Developer portal or API catalog for external consumers (Kong requires an add-on product for this)
WebSocket pub/sub or realtime messaging infrastructure (use Ably or Pusher)
best_when: You are managing a self-hosted or cloud-managed Kong instance and need agents to programmatically configure routing, auth, and traffic policies at runtime.
avoid_when: You need a fully managed, serverless API gateway with zero infrastructure to operate and a built-in developer portal.

auth.notes: Open-source Kong has no built-in Admin API authentication — operators must network-restrict the Admin API. Kong Enterprise adds RBAC with role-scoped tokens. Never expose the Admin API publicly without auth middleware.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Full open-source gateway features are free; self-hosted with no message or request limits
pricing.paid_tiers: Kong Konnect from ~$250/mo adds managed control plane, analytics, developer portal, and enterprise plugins
pricing.notes: Open-source version is fully functional for most use cases. Enterprise features like advanced RBAC, OpenID Connect plugin, and Vitals analytics require Konnect or Enterprise license.

performance.rate_limits: Admin API: no built-in rate limit (operator-configured); data plane proxy throughput is hardware/configuration dependent and can handle tens of thousands of RPS

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Returns JSON error objects with message fields. Validation errors list which fields failed. Some plugin configuration errors return opaque 500s that require log inspection for root cause.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Resources can be created with explicit IDs (UUID) making PUT operations idempotent. POST without an ID generates a new resource. Agents should use explicit IDs for safe retry.
known_agent_gotchas: The Admin API has no authentication in open-source Kong — agents must ensure the Admin API is only accessible within a trusted network or behind an auth proxy
Plugin configuration schemas vary significantly between plugins; agents generating plugin configs must validate against the specific plugin schema, not a generic structure
Route and service names must be unique within a workspace; agents creating routes dynamically must handle name conflicts or use UUIDs as identifiers
DBless mode (declarative config) does not support the Admin API write operations — agents must use deck or declarative YAML instead
Kong Enterprise and open-source have different plugin availability; agents must check which plugins are licensed before attempting to enable them

security_score_components.security_notes: Open-source Admin API has no authentication by default — this is a critical operational risk. Operators must restrict network access. Enterprise RBAC significantly improves security posture. TLS on proxy ports is fully supported.
